title: Unit 12: Graph Traversal
description: Learn how to traverse graphs (sets of vertices connected by edges) using queue-based BFS and stack-based DFS.

topics.title: 12.1 & 12.2 Breadth & Depth First Search
topics.description: BFS explores neighbors level-by-level using a Queue. DFS explores deep along paths before backtracking using recursion.

complexity.time: O(V + E) where V is vertices and E is edges
complexity.space: O(V) for visited set and data structure

ispo.desc: Accepts start node index, adjacency list 'adj', and visited array trackers.
Inserts the source start node into the traversal queue.
Instantiates a Queue in memory to store vertex traversal frontiers.
Declares node variable to store active dequeued vertex.
Declares neighbor variable inside iteration loop.
Drives queue-loop. Dequeues node, inspects adjacency index list, updates visited flags, and enqueues unvisited neighbors.
Prints visited vertices sequentially in BFS layout to standard output.

quiz.q: Which data structure is typically used internally or explicitly to implement Breadth First Search?

topics.title: 12.3 Best First Search
topics.description: An informed heuristic search that explores adjacent nodes based on evaluation cost, using a PriorityQueue (Min-Heap) to extract the most promising node next.

complexity.time: O(V log V + E log V)

ispo.desc: Accepts start, target destination, and graph adjacency structure.
Defines class representation Node containing vertex index and heuristic cost.
Allocates PriorityQueue and visited Boolean tracker array.
Declares u node pointer to hold popped node of lowest cost.
Iterates. Dequeues lowest-cost node, halts if target is met, and adds unvisited neighbors to PriorityQueue.
Outputs vertex indexes sequentially to standard output.

quiz.q: What data structure is utilized in Best First Search to sort nodes by heuristic cost dynamically?

topics.title: 12.4 Breadth First Traversal of a Graph
topics.description: Perform BFS traversal on a graph. BFS explores nodes level by level using a queue.

ispo.desc: Accepts vertices count V and graph representation adj.
Allocates result arraylist, visited boolean flags tracker, and BFS traversal queue.
Declares node variable to store current dequeued vertex.
Traverses level by level. Enqueues unvisited neighbors and logs visited values.
Returns list containing the traversal path.

quiz.q: What is the starting node index assumed in this BFS traversal code?

topics.title: 12.5 DFS for Disconnected Graph
topics.description: Perform DFS traversal on a graph that may contain disconnected components. Loop through all vertices and call DFS helper if unvisited.

ispo.desc: Accepts vertices count V and graph representation adj.
Allocates visited boolean tracker array in memory.
Iterates through all vertices in graph. Initiates DFS traversal for any vertex not yet visited, handling isolated components.
Recurses down adjacent unvisited branches.
Prints node index value to standard output.

quiz.q: How does this implementation ensure that vertices in disconnected graph components are all visited?
o: By iterating through all vertices [0...V-1] and calling DFS for any unvisited node

topics.title: 12.6 Detect Negative Cycle (Bellman-Ford)
topics.description: Detect whether a weighted directed graph contains a negative-weight cycle. Relax every edge V-1 times; if any edge can still be relaxed on one extra pass, a negative cycle exists. Distances start at 0 so a cycle reachable from any vertex is caught.
topics.cheatSheet:
Relax all E edges V-1 times.
Then do ONE more pass: if any edge still improves dist, a negative cycle exists.
Init all dist[] = 0 to detect a cycle anywhere in the graph.

ispo.desc: Accepts vertex count V and an edge list, each entry {src, dest, weight}.
Allocates the distance array, initialized to 0 so a cycle reachable from any vertex is detected.
Runs the standard Bellman-Ford relaxation: tightens every edge V-1 times.
Performs one additional relaxation pass; any further improvement proves a negative cycle.
Returns true the moment an edge still relaxes (negative cycle present).
Returns false when no edge can be relaxed further (no negative cycle).

quiz.q: After running the V-1 relaxation passes, how does Bellman-Ford prove a negative cycle exists?
o: It sorts the edges and checks for duplicates
It does one more relaxation pass; if any edge still reduces a distance, a negative cycle is present
It counts the number of visited vertices
It runs a BFS from every vertex
